body_txt: Almost the first thing that everybody knows that about Wizards and Witches is that they turn people into amphibians . It's usually frogs and toads, with the occasional salamander or newt ( possibly for use in potions ), but your results may vary. It's their standard party trick, the sorcerous equivalent of pulling a rabbit out of a hat . This is so well-known that anybody faced with a Wicked Witch is likely to beg her not to do this, at which point she'll probably do something much worse instead. In its "traditional" straight form, this trope is found primarily in cartoons and works directly based on fairytales; otherwise, especially in more parodic works, it's a common source of jokes, deconstructions, and other played-with forms — in some corners of media these notably outnumber its straight use. A variant found primarily in video games treats this as a variant of the Forced Transformation , with "turn to frog" spells serving essentially as a heavy debuff of target characters. Common ways to play with this trope include inversions, where a human character is actually a transformed frog; characters threatening to turn someone into a toad as a clearly empty or sarcastic threat; un-cursed characters retaining unfortunate habits such as a taste for flies; and Magic Misfires , where a caster flubbing a spell accidentally turns a bystander or themselves into a small amphibious animal. Whether the victim turns back when the spellcaster dies varies. A common variant, with reference to the tale of The Frog Prince , is that the spell can be broken by True Love's Kiss . Subversions usually opt for a mouse rather than an amphibian, playing on the association of witches and cats . Sub-Trope of Forced Transformation . See also Amphibian Assault and Amphibian at Large .
